content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Prompting for Robust Code Generation in Large Language Models",
  "problem": "Current code generation models often fail to generalize when minor variations in natural language instructions are introduced. This leads to brittle performance and reduced reliability in real-world developer scenarios.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are deployed for code generation in diverse and dynamic environments, users naturally phrase tasks in numerous, sometimes ambiguous ways. Ensuring models are robust to such instruction variability is crucial for adoption and utility. Yet, most prompting research focuses on optimizing or refining a single 'canonical' prompt rather than stressing models with realistic counterfactuals.",
  "method": "We propose 'Counterfactual Prompting,' where, for each code generation task, we generate a set of semantically equivalent but syntactically diverse prompts using backtranslation, paraphrasing, and learned transformation networks. These prompt sets are used during inference and/or few-shot exemplars, exposing the model to the natural variation in user queries. We hypothesize that this explicit prompt multiplicity will regularize model behavior, reduce overfitting to prompt phrasing, and yield more robust code outputs.",
  "experiment_plan": "We test our method on HumanEval, MBPP, and CodeContests, comparing against standard single-prompt and prompt-engineered baselines. Evaluation metrics include pass@k and robustness to prompt paraphrasing, measuring code correctness and consistency across prompt variations."
}
